item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
is it just me, or would the name "Akul'hil" be pronounced like an oddly-accented "alcohol"?

This spawned an interesting alternative interpretation:
TBOA is just some bros all about getting their drink on. And if they are a little hard to understand, that's just because you aren't listening right! I'm not slurring, you're slurring! See, oss-offs-offisir, my bros know what I'm sayin'!

Some frosh can't hang like a bro and wind up in a puddle on the floor. But stay cool, little brother! When you can't keep it together, find the nearest bro still standing upright and do whatever he says. He'll tell you if someone needs their ass kicked.

Of course, this means the redacted -1 phrase is "Chug! Chug! Chug!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The failure of Hadraniel-Alpha constitutes the immediate detonation of Area-███'s on-site thermonuclear device.

Grammatically speaking, this means that Hadraniel-Alpha's failure literally is the nuke detonating. Did you mean something more like "if H-A fails, then the nuke must be detonated"? If so, you want something like "requires" instead of "constitutes", or this needs to be rephrased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But the security clearance required is specifically for 4/1537, which means that if you have the ability to access that information, you are indeed in the compartment who needs to know and thus the blackboxes shouldn't be there. If you're any other level 4, you wouldn't be able to get into that section at all, making blackboxing anything within it redundant. Or at least that was my understanding of how it's supposed to work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Compartmentalization. A Level 4 might be authorized to know the general information about SCP's they aren't directly responsible for, but some details it just makes sense to keep on a need-to-know basis. Limiting the number of people who know which of the hundreds of foundation sites has this particular artifact of doom is just another layer of security.

Also, security clearances go to 5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get blackboxing the SCP designation in the main part of the protocols since you shouldn't need that info unless you've got security level 4, but why is it that after putting in proper clearance, things are still blackboxed and redacted? I'd think that would defeat the purpose of requiring security clearance at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This "spoiler" just leaves me with more questions than answers.

For example...
You should really answer these questions

1. Who's daughter?
2. That still doesn't tell me why she's radioactive.
3. Why is the designation blackboxed? If there's no reason for it, then don't blackbox it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the shoggoth idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-voting on this. There are some nice bits, but the cult seems too generic, and the whole thing lacks a final punch.

These things are crawling around me. If they had eyes, I assume they'd be looking at me. They want to know what they can do for me. Two weeks ago they were all my friends; now they're piles of mush.

This is the best part. If more of the article were like it, I'd upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote, because you manage to tell a decent story with it even though the idea seems a bit generic as monsters that kill stuff.

something you wrote which bugged the crap out of me:

"The variety of shapes that SCP-1537-A entities can assume is virtually limitless"

-can they change into shapes in the 4th dimension? Can they change into a tesseract? Probably not. "Limitless" is very uncalled for here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good to see my superpower of starting new pages in article comments still holds up. :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good example of why not to redact in containment procedures. It makes sense to have a high-order protocol hidden for security clearance, but not to withhold information from those personnel.

I'm not a big fan of the "TBOA" nomenclature. I suggest giving them a hyphenated designation under the SCP heading, especially since, if I logic this right, the Brotherhood are not human.

All the mentions of -B's giving "attack orders" to -A's, and what the offensive capabilities of -A's are, just makes this a funky language that turns people into Pokemon. Dittos, specifically.

SCP blackboxes doesn't really have enough going for it. (And having looked at your spoiler, I'm still not sure what any of it means.) The addenda though are nicely illustrative of what's going on, and I didn't anticipate the 1527 link, which is kind of neat. This should definitely be able to stand on its own merits, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed "following two weeks of exposure" to "two weeks after exposure", as the former indicates that a subject has been continuously exposed to the SCP for two weeks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this honestly just holds too much back and uses such a generic group for the thing behind it for me to like. It's all vaguely doomy with no personalitiy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That definitely improves it for me. I'm still iffy on how the effective redaction in the containment methods sits with me. I guess it could be automated, just hit the button that says "Hadraniel-Alpha", then if that doesn't work, hit the Bye-Bye button…I dunno. Still need to replace "constitutes" with "necessitates" or something similar. As it's written now, that line means that setting off the Big Nuke IS MADE OF the failure of Hadraniel-Alpha, and that's clearly not what's meant.

Downvote removed in any event. Sitting on an upvote ATM, I'll take another read after I've thought about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ SPOILERS
– hide block

The blackboxed SCP is the daughter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Density now changed to durability.

2. So far it only becomes violent if attacked or ordered to, and if it does try to destroy its cell, they'll just incinerate it.

3. The people containing the thing will already know what it is.

4. Hadraniel-Alpha is explained in the Clearance 5 secure file that is redacted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"SCP 1537 DASH BLACKBOX". What. How are we supposed to contain it if we can't even refer to it? Why in the world would this be blackboxed, especially within it's own containment procedures?

The way I understood it, the blackboxing was covering a numerical designation for the entity, like "SCP-1537-24," the appended number referring to its position on the list of recovered entities relating to the anomaly, which would both be unimportant to somebody trying to contain it and possibly be considered sensitive information. However, if it is as I had interpreted it, it should be formatted "SCP-1537-B-24" in order to clarify that it's referring to one of the affected persons and not a variation on the language itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand it at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You just nudged me into +1 territory with that log, but there's at least one glaring falsehood I want to point out:

Density is not necessarily a measure of strength. Mercury (13,534 kg/m3) is more dense than A36 structural steel (7,800 kg/m3).

Seriously, I was tempted to downvote for that line alone. Plz Fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Loved this. Loved the 1527 link, loved the new GOI, loved the new possible character. I pick up strong cthulhu mythos vibes here and I love it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Density does not = strength. Lead is both denser than steel and weaker.

Keeping an SCP that can form it's body into shapes that are both pointy and as strong as steel in a concrete cell is a bad idea.

"SCP 1537 DASH BLACKBOX". What. How are we supposed to contain it if we can't even refer to it? Why in the world would this be blackboxed, especially within it's own containment procedures?

Ok, laughing gas failed, we need Contingency-Hadraniel-Alpha! Which is not listed, linked to or even redacted anywhere.

"…failure of Hadraniel-Alpha constitutes the immediate detonation…" It may necessitate the immediate detonation, etc. It's very unlikely that it constitutes it.

Otherwise a serviceable concept, and the interview and document bits are better than average, but -1 as written.

:edit: I left off the 's', for savings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I like this. Uses the GOC in a way that doesn't make me hate their very existence, and some manner of new religion that is not incomprehensibly evil, and instead seems to just want to help. No bad guys here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not really common to use the GOC in this way. I like the way you handled the tool. +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tone's right, Grammar seems well (I'm not the best detecting this but I noticed nothing wrong about it), The hook's brilliant, the addendums about the GoC give some interesting background of an agent destined to destroy uhm parathreats that became a parathreat on it's own…

I can only say one thing: +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wow much upvote.

Is this GoI(?) going to be an ongoing project? Because my only complaint at the moment Is a complete lack of background motivation.
